The code executed 19 actions in the previous sequence.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 132 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

But the white extension is only 4 cells wide (the player's width), and the top blue center is 2x2. So the white extension would need to be long enough to reach the top blue center.
Actually, I think the white extension grows when I press SPACE, not when I move. So the white extension is currently 4 cells long (the player's width). Pressing SPACE would extend it by 4 cells in the current direction.
So if I press SPACE while the white extension is pointing LEFT, it would extend LEFT by 4 cells. But the player is at the left edge of the board, so the white extension can't extend LEFT.
Let me try pressing SPACE while the white extension is pointing RIGHT. But the white extension is currently pointing LEFT. I need to move RIGHT to change the white extension's direction to RIGHT.
Actually, wait. The white extension's direction is determined by the last movement. If I move RIGHT, the white extension would point RIGHT. If I move LEFT, the white extension would point LEFT. If I move UP, the white extension would point UP. If I move DOWN, the white extension would point DOWN.
So if I move RIGHT once, the white extension would point RIGHT. Then if I press SPACE, the white extension would extend RIGHT by 4 cells.
But the player is at cols 0-3. If I move RIGHT once, the player goes to cols 1-4. The white extension would be at col 4 (rightmost column), pointing RIGHT. Pressing SPACE would extend the white extension RIGHT by 4 cells, so the white extension would be at cols 4-7.

Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions by moving back and forth. Let me try a different approach. Instead of moving the player to the frame, maybe I can extend the white extension to reach the frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 24-27, cols 0-3
# Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
# Need to get to the frame to collect the blue centers
# Move RIGHT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to adjacent, then SPACE

# RIGHT x40 to get to cols 40-43
result = action(['RIGHT']*40)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT x40:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Move RIGHT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT x4 to cols 36-39 (inside the frame), then SPACE.
RIGHT x40: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 40-43
LEFT x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white LEFT
After LEFT x4, the player is at cols 36-39. The white extension is at col 36 (leftmost column), pointing LEFT. Pressing SPACE would extend the white extension LEFT from col 36, which is inside the frame. This would cover cols 35, 34, 33, 32, etc., which would overlap with the blue center at cols 33-34.
But the blue center at cols 37-38 is inside the player (cols 36-39), so it might already be collected.
Actually, I think the blue centers are collected when the white extension overlaps with them, not when the player overlaps with them. So if the player is at cols 36-39 and the blue center is at cols 37-38, the blue center is inside the player, but not necessarily collected.
Let me try this approach: RIGHT x40, DOWN x4, LEFT x4, SPACE.
But wait, the player is at cols 0-3. RIGHT x40 would move the player to cols 40-43. But the board is 64x64, so cols 40-43 is valid.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. Let me just try this approach and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 24-27, cols 0-3
# Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
# Need to get to the frame to collect the blue centers
# Move RIGHT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to adjacent, then SPACE

# RIGHT x40 to get to cols 40-43
result = action(['RIGHT']*40)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT x40:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
